excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "The Special Class Is Great!" -- As a result of increasing my English exposure time: I'm currently attending a language school
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme "The Special Class Is Great!"

-- As a Result of Increasing My English Exposure Time

I'm currently attending

a language school called "CELLA" in Talamban, Cebu.

I signed a 12-week (3-month) contract,

and I'm currently in my 6th week.

As I mentioned in my earlier articles,

I have a total of 8 class periods.

(* Each period is 45 minutes)

Normally, classes start at 8 AM

and end at 5:10 PM,

but 2 periods are self-study time (everyone has free periods),

so in my case, my classes end at 4:20 PM.

So I use that time

for my daily running and things like that.

However, when I finished the 5th week,

my desire to spend more time with English grew stronger, and on top of that, a big piece of work for my masu brand "FOMUS" was wrapped up, so I had a bit more breathing room.

So starting this week, I signed up for the "Special Class" -- a vocabulary-building course that anyone can join by applying.

It consists of a 15-minute vocabulary test starting at 7 AM,

and a vocabulary-focused class from 7 PM to 7:45 PM in the evening.

I wrote about this yesterday too!

This runs from Monday to Friday (no evening class on Friday),

and since the weekday curfew is 10 PM,

I basically don't go out for fun on weekdays.

So as a result, I end up either studying English or working on FOMUS.

About this Special Class --

it seems like a different teacher handles it each day,

and the teachers who are especially good at teaching (the popular ones) run the classes.

So compared to my daytime classes,

the English is spoken at a faster pace,

but because they're better at teaching,

I end up understanding it anyway.

It's just one extra 45-minute class,

but for me it was a big change.

* By the way, the test for this evening class is given the next morning

In my case, I basically

don't have homework in my regular classes,

so I can set aside about an hour for vocabulary study.

The reason I don't have homework is

because I'm not doing textbook-based classes (I've customized them to focus on talking).

Everyone has their own approach,

but in my case, I need to acquire English I can actually use in practice, so I feel like textbook-based learning is something I could do back in Japan.

That's exactly why I'm increasing my time speaking and listening in English.

On top of that, I'm also consciously keeping my interactions with other Japanese students to a relative minimum.

To be honest, since it's spring break for universities right now,

there are quite a few Japanese college students here.

That said, I didn't come here

to chat with Japanese college students,

so I don't go out of my way to talk to them.

During free time in the evenings, I'm basically

working on FOMUS stuff.

* I do envy the college students who can just enjoy the moment without worrying about anything lol

The time I have to focus on my work

is different from when I'm in Japan,

so I need to be even more efficient,

and I'm experimenting with that every day.

Lately, I've been getting replies from models I reached out to, little by little, and I'm handling those responses,

while also continuing to send emails to new models.

Honestly, I wish I could just let AI handle this

and have it send them automatically,

but I'm sending each one individually, with their name included.

Man,

every day flies by so fast,

it's amazing lol

Anyway, by increasing my time with English,

things are boosted even further.

I'll keep pushing forward!

https://youtu.be/bKEHJJvU11w?si=5Mr-KBRWokh8jueE

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Moving Forward with Preparations." -- Plans for after June: This time I want to write about what comes after my study abroad in Cebu. In my case, I'll be finishing up in Cebu
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme "Moving Forward with Preparations."

-- Plans for After June

This time I want to write about what comes after my study abroad in Cebu.

In my case, I'll be done with my activities in Cebu and back in Japan by the end of April, and my activities in Ireland start in July, so if I don't get the paperwork moving now, I might not make it in time.

So I've already received approval for my Ireland working holiday visa, and as soon as I return to Japan, I'll be sending documents and my passport to the Irish Embassy.

The reason it's July is

because there's a rule that you have to travel at least 2 months after sending your passport and other documents to the embassy.

So in my case, since I brought my passport to Cebu, the earliest I can go is the second week of July.

And on September 6, 7, and 8, the Medoc Marathon is coming up in Bordeaux, France.

* This is already arranged

* Apparently the spots filled up right after registration opened

So,

one option I was considering was

attending a language school in Ireland for 7-8 weeks from mid-July to the end of August while also expanding FOMUS,

but since I have a whole year on the working holiday visa, I'm thinking maybe I should do a longer language study.

Honestly, there's no way I'll become fluent in English in just a few months, so I need to keep using it consistently.

Just like in Cebu, going to school while expanding FOMUS might actually be the better way to get more people to know about the brand.

On top of that, since I'll be doing a homestay,

it'll be relatively easy to interact with local people.

My goals for the working holiday in Ireland

are mainly three things:

1. Bring the FOMUS brand to more people

2. Learn Irish culture and apply it to FOMUS

3. Be able to do business in English as second nature by age 30 -- only one more year

Considering all of this,

I need to carefully think about how long to study in Ireland.

Also, even after language school ends, I can stay in Europe including Ireland for a year, so I could take my time visiting and scouting different places in Europe.

* Flights to anywhere in Europe are cheap from Ireland

Also, if the European environment suits me well,

next year I could get a working holiday visa for a European country other than Ireland,

and spend another year

expanding FOMUS across Europe.

Anyway, you can never have too many options.

-- There's a Country I Want to Visit

On a personal note, there's a country I want to visit.

That's Spain.

During my Ireland study abroad, I want to focus on studying,

and travel around outside of the school period.

So if I were to do 3 months of language school,

my trip to Spain would be pushed back quite a bit.

So,

what I'm currently thinking is,

on my way to Ireland,

I could stop by Spain first and get the FOMUS brand awareness work and sightseeing done ahead of time.

If I can do "masu x paella" in Spain,

then when I go to other European countries,

"Oh, so that's how you use this masu! Then how should we use it?"

-- it could become

one of the options that inspires people to think that way.

Staying in Spain for 2-3 weeks

and checking things out sounds pretty interesting.

Anyway, it might not be perfectly organized, but while I'm here in Cebu, I've been able to create time to carefully think about my schedule from June onwards.

Being in Cebu and getting stimulated by this environment,

it's really tricky to decide

how many weeks to spend in Ireland.

I could just stay at a hotel in Ireland, but when I think about the hassle of cooking and the costs, doing a homestay actually turns out to be surprisingly cheaper.

There's still a lot to think about.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Drinking Parties Connect People." -- Fridays are for hanging out! My 6th week of language school wrapped up today. This marks the halfway point of my Cebu activities.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Drinking Parties Connect People."

-- Fridays Are for Hanging Out!

My 6th week of language school wrapped up today.

This marks the halfway point of my Cebu activities.

Honestly,

each day flies by at an incredible pace,

but in return, every day is packed and fulfilling.

The big thing is

that all my roommates are from different countries,

so the only common language is English,

and if you want to communicate,

you have to use English.

So,

after school, I headed to a bar near the school with my roommates.

We've been there so many times that

the owner recognizes our faces now lol

This bar is the closest one to the school, so tons of students and teachers gather there on Fridays.

So if you go there,

you naturally end up communicating in English.

Add some drinks to the mix,

and the communication flows even smoother.

It's something I look forward to every week.

I asked my teachers

to skip the textbooks

and make the classes conversation-focused.

I fully understand

that textbooks are important,

but even more than that, what I need right now

is actual speaking skills.

Plus, I hate to say it, but

you can study textbooks back in Japan!! lol

As for conversation practice, online English lessons exist these days,

but having actually used online lessons myself,

I can say that talking face-to-face in person is overwhelmingly more effective.

Honestly, whether it's online or in person,

you can have the same kind of conversation,

but when the physical distance is shorter,

maybe you can concentrate better.

So,

out of my 8 class periods,

4 of them are conversation-centered classes without textbooks.

They're more practical, hands-on classes.

That's exactly why, even at drinking events,

I can naturally communicate in English, and going forward in my overseas life,

I'm gradually building up at least the minimum skills I need.

Being able to practice what I've learned on weekends

is something you can only do in person.

On top of that, I get to talk with people at the bar

whom I normally wouldn't talk to at school,

so I make more friends.

That's a huge plus!

Starting in July,

I'm planning about 3 months of language school in Ireland, and I'm sure drinking parties will be an important tool there too.

Once again,

I'm grateful I was born with a body that can handle alcohol.

* Though my money disappears like magic in return

https://www.notion.so

-- All Sorts of Projects Moving Forward

While enjoying life in Cebu,

I'm also pushing FOMUS projects forward simultaneously.

Every day I send messages to tons of Filipino models, get excited over the handful of replies I receive,

and then share more details with them.

Just because they reply

doesn't mean we can do a shoot right away,

so I'm steadily chipping away at this.

* Lately I've been having models introduce me to other models

On top of that, I'm also pushing forward the music project for FOMUS's character "KUKU."

My role is creating the backgrounds for the videos we release every week.

For the backgrounds, I use "AI image generation,"

which is my area of expertise and also one of my jobs.

Sure, anyone can create illustrations using AI, but by mixing in a masu-wearing character that AI can't generate and timeless music,

it might not go massively viral,

but it will become an asset-building project going forward.

Monetization is still a ways off.

I'm also preparing for the

crowdfunding campaign launching in April.

We Want to Bring "FOMUS," a Traditional Masu Brand, to the World!!

And then there's the prep for

FOMUS's digital art exhibition, which starts 3 days after I return to Japan.

I've got a lot of projects on my plate,

but everything is for the purpose of bringing

the FOMUS brand to more people.

I need people to get interested in FOMUS

through channels beyond just masu itself.

Every single day, when it comes to business,

the anxiety never goes away,

but this is the path I chose.

I'll keep moving forward

to make the path I chose the right one.

PS

I can finally run 8km now.

For me, that's an incredible amount of progress!

* When I started, 3km in 25 minutes was my limit! lol

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Picnics Are the Best." -- Masu x Sweets in Cebu: So it's Saturday of my 6th week. Since there are no classes, I need to create opportunities to speak English on my own,
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Picnics Are the Best."

-- Masu x Sweets in Cebu

So it's Saturday of my 6th week.

Since there are no classes,

I need to create opportunities to speak English on my own, but for the weekends in March, I've basically decided not to do local Masu Photo shoots and instead spend time socializing with students and teachers from the language school.

There's a reason for this -- when I arrived in February, more than 10 Japanese college students enrolled at the same time,

and there weren't many students from other countries.

So on days off,

the other students would go

sightseeing together as a group of Japanese students,

but I wasn't interested in that,

so I didn't join them.

Also, for my FOMUS Masu Photo activities, I had set a goal of photographing 10 local subjects in February, and I successfully shot with 15 people by the end of February, so for March weekends, I'm dedicating at least one of the two days to spending time with school people.

As a result, the ratio of Japanese students has dropped a bit,

and I'm making a conscious effort to talk with non-Japanese students and teachers -- and it's working great.

It's not that I refuse to talk to Japanese people,

but with just a little awareness, I've built an environment where I'm constantly using English.

So,

today I went to a mall within walking distance with one of my teachers and her manager.

The teacher looked up a sweets shop for us,

and we decided to do a masu sweets session!

I didn't think I could eat 3 dishes on my own,

and for the FOMUS collab promotion,

the teacher brought the manager along too.

* I covered the bill

In the end, I'm glad there were 3 of us given the amount of food.

I got to do a FOMUS masu sweets session in Cebu,

and during the wait time I got to chat with the teachers in English -- two birds with one stone for me!

For them too,

free sweets -- so everyone's happy!

Being able to create opportunities like this

is because I talk about my work during class,

and the teachers end up introducing me to all sorts of things.

This is a perk of doing language study

while running a business.

* Shoots with local people too

Anyway,

I managed to get some great photos.

That said,

this wasn't an official collaboration with the shop --

we just bought items from the store

and arranged them ourselves,

so this one is more of a concept image.

I really want to do an actual collab though!!

-- Off to the Picnic

We went for sweets at noon.

Then at 3 PM, about 10 teachers and 10 students headed to a hill within walking distance from the school for a picnic!

Everyone brought snacks to share

and we enjoyed conversation.

Of course there were some Japanese people,

but they weren't the majority,

so naturally everyone was using English,

which was great for me!

**The key to improving English

is how much you use it on your days off.**

In February, I spent every day off on Masu Photo shoots,

forcing myself into environments where I had to use English.

In March, I'm creating time with teachers on days off

to build English-speaking environments.

Today was actually

a day where I used English all day long.

I'm grateful to the teachers for creating opportunities like this,

and by jumping in and having fun,

I got some great English training!

What's more,

after the picnic, I got to know some Taiwanese and Korean girls, and we all went for a massage together.

After everyone got their massages,

we all went to Jollibee (think of it as the McDonald's over here).

When it comes to Taiwan, I stayed there for a month last year

and even did the crazy thing of cycling around the entire island, so I feel like we bonded faster.

I realized that sharing experiences you've actually gone out, seen, and lived through is really important.

And so,

Saturday of week 6 turned out to be a really meaningful day.

Tomorrow I have no particular plans!

I think I'll catch up on all the work that's been piling up!

* That might change though!

A lighter evacuation plan for today

title_en: Going to Meet an Exciting Future
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Going to Meet an Exciting Future." -- Overseas Scouting and Work: I'm now in my 7th week in Cebu! On this Sunday, I didn't go anywhere -- just worked and studied English on the school rooftop.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Going to Meet an Exciting Future."

-- Overseas Scouting and Work

I'm now in my 7th week in Cebu!

On this Sunday, I didn't go anywhere --

just worked and studied English on the school rooftop.

I was prepping for events after Cebu

and researching my activities in

Ireland starting in July.

Originally, I was planning to start language school and FOMUS expansion in Dublin (Ireland's capital) from the 2nd or 3rd week of July,

but for a certain reason, I need to do a Masu Photo shoot in America at some point this year, so I'm thinking of getting that done before heading to Ireland.

But America is incredibly expensive,

so I can't stay long.

#saving-is-a-must

On top of that, as I wrote in my previous article,

I want to check out Spain at an early stage,

and if I fit all of this together nicely...

In early July:

Tokyo -> Los Angeles: 3-5 day stay

Los Angeles -> New York: 3-5 day stay

New York -> Barcelona: 5-7 day stay

Barcelona -> Madrid: 5-7 day stay

Madrid -> Dublin (Ireland): Late July

That's roughly what the schedule is looking like.

Honestly, since it's July, prices go up across the board,

and with the weak yen it's going to be tough,

but if I miss this chance, my opportunity to see the world in my 20s just keeps getting pushed back, so that's why I came up with this schedule.

That said, nothing's confirmed yet.

You might think all that travel would cost a fortune, but the flights altogether come to about 200,000 yen.

That's because I'm searching for the cheapest dates for every single leg.

Of course, they're all budget airlines.

* Right now I can't afford to spend money on flight comfort

* I'd rather put that money toward strengthening the brand

Also, since America is expensive,

I'm keeping those stays shorter while staying a bit longer in relatively affordable Spain.

For Spain, I simply want to see the Sagrada Familia, and I also want to do Masu Photo shoots there.

On top of that, I want to do "masu x paella" locally.

If I can get America and Spain done in July,

then from August to October I can attend language school,

which will make it way easier to make European friends,

and by doing a homestay,

I can keep costs lower than staying at a hotel.

I've already identified the language school in Ireland, and like Cebu, I'm thinking 12 weeks (3 months).

However, the homestay arranged by the school only covers up to 8 weeks (2 months) in the plan, and from week 9 onward the price jumps dramatically, so before that I'll need to either find a share house or move to a hostel.

Personally, for the remaining 4 weeks after the homestay ends,

I'd like to stay at a hostel near the school

and spend that time socializing with the people staying there.

Because I'm attending language school in Cebu,

I can kind of picture what studying in Ireland will be like.

* Obviously it won't be exactly the same though

On top of that, in early September, I'll be running in the Medoc Marathon in France.

That'll overlap a bit with language school,

but there's nothing I can do about that.

* September 5-10 is Bordeaux

In 2024, I declared that I'd go overseas, expand FOMUS, and study English,

and at this rate, it looks like I'll actually follow through.

That said, as things stand,

I won't be able to stay for a full year,

so I also need to push forward with the crowdfunding campaign starting in April.

We Want to Bring "FOMUS," a Traditional Masu Brand, to the World!!

Bottom line, to carry out activities overseas,

you need a lot of preparation and money.

That goes for me personally,

and for the FOMUS brand too --

it's extremely important to provide more value

and generate more profit.

I'll keep working on raising money and building connections.

And since I need to be able to raise money from people overseas too, I'm going all-in on learning English.

With that,

I'll give my best in week 7 of classes starting tomorrow!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Masu Photo Connects the World." -- A Shoot Etched in Memory: Today, after my classes, I went to Mactan Island, right next to Cebu Island. The purpose was a photoshoot.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Masu Photo Connects the World."

-- A Shoot Etched in Memory

Today, after my classes,

I went to Mactan Island, right next to Cebu Island.

The purpose was a photoshoot.

This shoot came about because

the very first model I photographed

in my first week in Cebu later told me,

"The photos you took last time were so good -- I'd love for you to do a commemorative shoot for my 20th birthday."

That's the offer I received.

I never expected, as a photographer here in Cebu,

to be blessed with an opportunity like this.

When I arrived,

one of her friends came to pick me up.

And when I went into the room,

there was the model getting her hair and makeup done.

Since we'd already properly connected before,

it felt like hanging out with a friend.

Since this was a serious shoot,

she had even hired a hair and makeup artist.

That's something I should learn from.

* Usually the models do their own

Hair and makeup took about an hour,

and then the shoot began.

In my case,

I rarely shoot indoors,

but I did a solid, professional job.

She's active as a model,

so she must know tons of

other photographers,

yet she chose me -- a Japanese guy (foreigner) she'd only met once -- for her milestone 20th birthday photoshoot.

I'm genuinely happy about that.

Now, here's the important part --

Why was I chosen?

That's the question.

Honestly,

I'm confident in my ability to create the right vibe during shoots, my camera angles,

and my use of color tones.

I think I'm good at capturing both what the subject wants and what they subconsciously desire in a photo.

But,

let's be real --

there are plenty of people who can take great photos.

Despite that,

I got this offer,

and I think there are several reasons why.

It's important

to properly analyze this.

At this point, I think it comes down to:

1. The quality of the shoot met her standards

2. The schedule worked out

3. The atmosphere during the first shoot was great

4. The unique "Masu Photo" experience -- taking photos holding a masu that nobody else does

5. I was thorough with post-shoot communication

6. We're close in age

That's what I think.

When I actually asked

this model directly for her reasons,

she said it was 1 and 2.

But,

there are plenty of people who satisfy those two criteria.

What differentiates me beyond that are factors 3 through 6.

I know it sounds a bit presumptuous to say this myself, but

I don't shoot with the mindset of

"I want to take great photos."

Rather,

my stronger feeling is that I want them

to enjoy the shoot itself.

Because if I'm behind the camera, I can take great photos regardless.

Honestly, high-quality photos come down to

having a quality camera

and the photographer's sense, I think.

That's less about effort and more about

how many people you've shot with, one-on-one -- it's a matter of volume.

Then I combine that with my own sense and instincts.

I barely know anything about cameras themselves.

But the reason my color tones get praised is because the quality of my post-shoot editing is high.

If I keep going, this will get presumptuous even for a paid article, so I'll stop here lol

Regarding 4, Masu Photo -- it's a project born from FOMUS, and it would be meaningless for someone else to copy it.

But in this era where every person on earth has a smartphone and has become a photographer,

and where AI is also evolving,

Masu Photo shoots

will absolutely never be replaced by AI.

Because:

1. A masu with the FOMUS logo printed on it can't be AI-generated

2. It's an experience of touching Japanese traditional crafts

3. The memory of participating in a Masu Photo shoot and sharing time with MaSU

These memories are incredibly powerful,

and they'll become even more important in the future.

As the FOMUS brand grows,

the rarity value of Masu Photo (shot by MaSU) will increase,

and it will also benefit the people who participated in Masu Photo shoots at this early stage.

The Masu Photo experience

is something money can't buy.

* Because I carefully select participants

As for 5 and 6,

anyone who's done a shoot with me knows --

the vibe is super relaxed! lol

The atmosphere is laid-back,

but when it's time to shoot, I shoot properly.

Before and after the shoot,

I make sure to communicate well,

sharing the future I want to create with FOMUS

and listening to what the other person wants to do.

Also, since I'm in my late 20s,

younger models feel

at ease, which is a plus.

Plus, on my Instagram account, I post my face and

two-shots with models, so it's easy to position myself as someone they'd want to be photographed by.

All these elements come together

to make me a "photographer who sticks in people's memories."

* Of course, without the quality from point 1, you can't even function as a photographer in the first place, so that's essential.

If you only chase photo quality,

what awaits you is competition with AI.

Because I'm hands-on with cutting-edge AI,

my shooting style weaves in things AI can absolutely never create -- Japanese traditional crafts, memories, and human connections.

What's more,

the fact that Masu Photo is expanding not just in Japan but worldwide creates an overwhelming competitive advantage.

These hypotheses and validations aren't confirmed yet,

so I'll spend the next several years proving them.

-- FOMUS's Strengths

So far I've talked about my shooting style,

the rarity value of Masu Photo,

and what makes a photographer memorable.

What's even more interesting is that

FOMUS masu are owned by a select group of people in Japan, Taiwan, Malaysia, and the Philippines.

Currently the largest number of owners is in Japan,

and in Japan, people who already own a FOMUS masu

visit shops that carry FOMUS masu,

and travel with other masu holders.

In other words,

"I own a FOMUS masu"

"I'm connected to FOMUS"

-- these shared bonds create an environment (community) where people connect, and we're building that little by little.

In this era where many adults struggle to make new connections, FOMUS regularly hosts events where people toast with FOMUS masu.

Horizontal connections form there,

and FOMUS becomes a part of each person's story.

This too is something AI can't create,

and it simply takes time to build.

FOMUS may never go viral,

but instead, it has a strong, strong community,

and the masu serves its role as the vessel for toasting.

Honestly, the masu business alone

would run out of money, and nobody could copy it,

but it works because I'm also running other businesses like AI support, consulting, photography, and accessories alongside it.

Furthermore,

I'll be creating an international version of the Masu Photo book going forward,

so more companies and individuals who want to sponsor it will emerge.

The possibilities here are limitless!!

So today I rambled on about FOMUS project strengths and why I get chosen as a photographer.

People are surprised that I can do all this overseas as if it's normal, but from my perspective, the only thing that changed is the language -- from Japanese to English. I'm still having them hold masu.

Being in Cebu has really

clarified what elements will be essential in the coming era.

I'll keep pushing forward!!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Building the Skills I Need." -- Customizing My Classes: At the school I attend, after taking an initial level test, they make you purchase textbooks that match your level.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Building the Skills I Need."

-- Customizing My Classes

At the school I attend,

after taking an initial level test,

they make you purchase textbooks that match your level.

It's about 7-8 books, costing around 6,000 yen.

Purchasing them is mandatory.

I used those textbooks during my first week,

following them for my classes.

Textbooks are great for building reading and writing skills,

but when you have a teacher right in front of you who can speak English, it felt like a waste to reduce the time for speaking and listening -- especially since I came here to learn business English.

So I directly told my teachers,

"I want to learn through conversation without using textbooks,"

and that's what I requested.

Starting from the 2nd week, several teachers

switched to conversation-centered classes without textbooks.

Now in week 7, I'm only using textbooks for

2-3 class periods, and more than half are conversations with my teachers.

Even in those conversation classes,

we basically keep things business-oriented.

Some teachers understand business and some don't but are interested in it,

so the topics change accordingly.

Communicating those ideas effectively in English

gives me the feeling that I'm strengthening exactly what I need.

With teachers who can talk business,

I have those conversations while getting corrected on grammar and pronunciation.

With teachers who are interested in business,

I talk about the FOMUS business model

and organizational structure in English.

I talk about business,

and when my English is wrong, the teacher corrects it.

For me, it creates opportunities to discuss business in English, and it doubles as English study.

With one teacher in particular,

I've been working on pronunciation practice.

I think about what skills I'm currently lacking

and what can only be done right here, right now,

and customize my classes accordingly.

By the way, most students, naturally,

use textbooks.

I've been doing things my own way --

frequently changing teachers, skipping textbooks,

and customizing my classes --

but for someone like me who knows for certain he'll be using English for business, this approach works much better.

When I come across words I don't know, I look them up on the spot and practice using them, which helps them stick.

Being able to take classes proactively

rather than passively

is partly because I'm here

as a businessman, not a college student.

I'm spending precious time in my 20s

here in Cebu,

devoting 3 months to learning English.

That's how essential English is for me right now.

-- Teachers Are People Too

Now,

one thing I've been conscious about since coming here is

making my teachers think,

"I want to teach this guy more!"

"I want to talk to him more!"

-- that's the kind of impression I try to make.

Because

teachers are people with emotions, not AI.

There's a 5-minute break between classes,

and I keep the conversation going right up until the last second.

The tricks I use include

lifting the teacher's mood,

active listening reactions,

the listener's actions,

and just being personable.

From my life experience so far,

I enjoy talking and listening about

people I'm interested in and topics that interest me.

Lately, as a listening exercise,

I've been asking my teachers to share

their stories about why they chose their careers.

If you just do regular classes,

it turns into just going through textbooks, and teachers get bored too.

That's why, during my classes,

I break the mold

and skip the textbooks.

From the teachers' perspective,

"Don't use the textbook"

is apparently a rare request from students,

so they find it refreshing.

Plus, since I'm also running a business,

teachers actually come up with topics and themes for my classes.

* I also take thorough notes

In other words, the teachers are spending time outside of my class hours

thinking about how to structure my lessons.

* Occupying their mindshare

I'm really grateful for that.

So,

to build the skills I need right now,

I'm customizing my classes and lifestyle.

That's the story.

I hope this is helpful for someone!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Using AI in Cebu." -- ChatGPT is incredibly useful: It's my 7th week in Cebu, and I've been using ChatGPT every single day.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Using AI in Cebu."

-- ChatGPT Is Incredibly Useful

It's my 7th week in Cebu,

and I've been using ChatGPT every single day.

How am I using it? Well,

I take photos of parts of the textbook I don't understand

and have it explain them to me.

It's fine to ask my teachers,

but for stuff that's not really worth asking about,

I use AI first to deepen my understanding.

Not long ago,

I used to type each word one by one into a translation app to look things up,

but now I can copy-paste text to translate it,

and as of this point [2024/03/20],

if you take a photo and type "translate this,"

it translates without even needing to type out the text.

Lately,

I've been taking the evening vocabulary-building class too,

and the teachers explain English words using English,

so there are still parts I don't fully understand.

So I use AI to

absorb the vocabulary info I need in advance.

That way,

I can better understand the conversations between students and teachers,

which ultimately improves my English skills.

On the work side,

I also have it check the messages I send to models

and translate messages I receive from models --

I leave that to AI as well.

Since I'm sending a massive number of emails every day,

AI is incredibly handy in these situations.

-- Becoming Someone Who Masters AI

In the coming era,

people whose work can be replaced by AI

are going to have it really tough.

That's why I'm mastering AI now

while creating various types of content.

This divide will create a huge gap.

In fact,

my work efficiency

has improved dramatically since I started using AI.

These are precious years in my 20s.

I can't waste them on unnecessary things.

I'll keep learning about AI going forward.

title_en: The Potential of Masu x Card Games
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "The Potential of Masu x Card Games." -- A card game that can be played worldwide: At the beginning of 2023, together with FOMUS members, using AI and the community, we created
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"The Potential of Masu x Card Games."

-- A Card Game That Can Be Played Worldwide

At the beginning of 2023,

together with FOMUS members,

using AI and the community, we created

the world's first card game you can play with a masu -- "SILVA." I brought it with me on this Cebu study abroad trip.

Check out SILVA here:

https://silva.official.ec/

There are 3 main reasons I created this card game:

1. When I thought about how to get kids and young people to experience masu, card games were the answer

2. We need to create things collaboratively using technology like AI going forward

3. Something that can be played worldwide (non-verbal communication)

There are plenty more reasons if I get into the details,

but that's the gist of it.

To spread SILVA globally,

using platforms like YouTube and other social media is a given,

but I also think it's important for me to be able to explain it in English myself.

So,

I used 3 of my class periods in one day

to practice explaining the card game "SILVA" in English.

Explaining it in English to each teacher one-on-one

was quite challenging,

but since I had translated the Japanese rulebook into English in advance,

I had them read that and went from there.

For the English translation,

I used ChatGPT

to translate it in an easy-to-understand way.

* Super useful

Each class period is only 45 minutes,

but I spent about 30 minutes explaining the rules,

and 15 minutes playing a match, then re-explained the finer rules in my own words.

It was actually my first time

playing a card game with someone from overseas,

but once they learned the rules,

it proved that this game can facilitate

interaction anywhere in the world.

I'd like to use class time next week

to further upgrade

how I explain it.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Going Out for Filipino Food." -- Fridays are full of socializing: After class, two Taiwanese girls, one Taiwanese guy, one Chinese guy, and me -- five of us headed to a Filipino food restaurant by the Cebu seaside.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Going Out for Filipino Food."

-- Fridays Are Full of Socializing

After class,

two Taiwanese girls, one Taiwanese guy, one Chinese guy, and me -- five of us headed to a Filipino food restaurant by the seaside in Cebu.

It's one of the fun parts of studying abroad.

And I love that I was the only Japanese person there!

But since everyone else was from Taiwan and China,

when they switch to Chinese,

I'm completely lost lol

That said,

I don't feel left out at all --

I actually enjoy that kind of environment.

When I speak in English, everyone uses English,

so it's not really an issue.

Anyway,

just a report that we went a little far out for dinner.

The cost was 250 pesos for transportation and 510 pesos for food.

* 1 peso is about 2.6 yen

Being able to have a delicious dinner with friends for less than 2,000 yen

is insanely cheap.

After that,

we headed to our regular bar near the school.

There were some Korean friends there,

and someone who works in the school cafeteria,

so more socializing happened.

There were groups of Japanese college students and working adults too,

but I'm not really into hanging out with other Japanese people (all the way here in Cebu), so I keep a comfortable distance.

* I do enjoy talking one-on-one though

Thanks to that, I can move freely between different groups on my own,

and when teachers show up, I jump into their conversations

and practice my English.

This agility and

communication ability

were skills I unknowingly developed

through 3 years of FOMUS activities

and my experiences as an address hopper,

dropping into unfamiliar places

all the time.

I now consider this one of my strengths.

Thanks to this, I'm building more connections

with teachers than with students.

* It's nice being able to have adult conversations

* Everyone loves anime and Shohei Ohtani

It's not like I'm going out of my way to plug FOMUS, but the teachers already know I'm a photographer and run a masu brand,

so at this point, there's really nothing left to explain lol

In this way,

in my daily life,

I'm conscious about spending my time with

international students and

teachers -- communicating with them.

I only have 3 months in Cebu.

Where you spend your time matters.

* That doesn't mean I avoid talking to Japanese people though

* I speak to them in English

So that's how every Friday,

beer in hand, I'm enjoying socializing

while learning English!
